summary: High‑energy Laguna Seca set with monster Other One jam and soaring vocals
blurb: The band delivers a high‑octane first set highlighted by Mississippi Half-Step and West L.A., then launches into a sprawling second set anchored by an explosive The Other One and soaring Morning Dew. Brent’s vocals are front‑and‑center, Phil’s bass is thick and prominent, and the drums/space segment builds a psychedelic 20‑minute monster jam.
review: July 31, 1988 at Laguna Seca Recreation Area was a birthday weekend to remember for Jerry Garcia and the Dead. The first set crackled with energy – Mississippi Half-Step Uptown Toodeloo and West L.A. Fadeaway showcased Brent’s vocal firepower while Bob Weir’s soulful Rooster solo (Little Red Rooster) earned plenty of praise. The second half turned into a marathon of improvisation: after a solid Hell In A Bucket the band dove into a monster 20‑minute stretch of Drums, Space and an unrelenting The Other One that left fans calling it “psychedelic depth” and “a magical jam.” Guest guitarist David Hidalgo added tasty licks on West L.A. and the Rooster solo, giving the night a unique flavor. Despite a few minor tape glitches (the Hell In A Bucket repeat) and a vocal strain from Jerry noted on one board, the overall performance was tight, happy, and high‑energy – a true showcase of the ’88 repertoire at its peak.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Four of five recordings describe the show in strongly positive terms ("monster performance", "happy high‑energy band", "magical", "explosive The Other One"). Only one recording is mixed, noting minor vocal issues but not detracting from overall excellence. Consistent praise across multiple sources warrants a solid positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Soundboard source with generally good clarity, captures the full set and highlights while only having minor technical glitches; widely regarded as the most complete and balanced capture of this show.

key_highlights: Explosive The Other One > Space jam lasting over 20 minutes
Brent Mydland’s powerful vocal delivery on multiple tunes
Guest guitarist David Hidalgo shines on West L.A. and Rooster solo
Phil Lesh’s bass is thick, prominent, and drives the entire performance

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia’s guitar remains gritty and earthy throughout, delivering solid leads; his vocals show strain on a few numbers but overall he stays in the pocket of the birthday‑weekend vibe.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh’s bass is front‑and‑center – described as ‘loads of Phil’, thick, bright, and adding depth to every groove.
band_performance.Bob: Weir’s rhythm work is tight and on point; his Rooster solo earns special kudos for soulful expression.
band_performance.Brent: Mydland dominates with powerful vocals and ever‑present keyboards, steering the band through high‑energy moments and key transitions.
band_performance.Mickey: Hart helps shape a psychedelic 20‑minute Drums/Space journey that fuels The Other One’s monster jam.
band_performance.Billy: Kreutzmann locks in with Hart, providing the driving force behind the expansive improvisations.
